key_messages: High pressure building through the end of the week will bring dry weather with a gradual increasing trend in temperatures.
Quiet weather and increasing temperatures are expected this upcoming weekend before another system bring showers and thunderstorms to the region early next week.
afd.raw:
000
FXUS61 KBTV 222346
AFDBTV

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Burlington VT
Issued by National Weather Service Albany NY
746 PM EDT Wed Jul 22 2026

.WHAT HAS CHANGED...
Forecast is on track.

&&

.KEY MESSAGES...
As of 225 AM EDT Wednesday...

1. High pressure building through the end of the week will 
bring dry weather with a gradual increasing trend in temperatures. 

2. Quiet weather and increasing temperatures are expected this 
upcoming weekend before another system bring showers and 
thunderstorms to the region early next week.

&&

.DISCUSSION...
As of 225 AM EDT Wednesday...

KEY MESSAGE 1: This afternoon marks the beginning of a period of 
quiet weather as a drier post-frontal air mass moves in and high 
pressure builds.  Any residual showers this afternoon will depart to 
the east by this evening, and the remainder of the day will be dry. 
West/northwest winds will continue to gust in the 15 to 30 mph range 
this afternoon before weakening after sunrise.  Temperatures over 
the next few days will undergo a gradual warming trend.  Highs 
Thursday and Friday will be in the 70s to low 80s, with highs 
warming to the low to mid 80s areawide by Saturday.  Dewpoints will 
be noticeably lower for the second half of the week compared to the 
last few days, making for a comfortable end of the week.  No 
precipitation is expected through Saturday. KEY MESSAGE 2: High 
pressure starts to shift to the east by the second half of the 
weekend, leading to increasing S/SW flow. The result will be warming 
temperatures, with highs to be mainly in the mid/upper 80s both 
Saturday and Sunday. Humidity doesn't look too bad  as dewpoints are 
expected to remain the upper 50s to mid 60s. Our next chance of 
precipitation arrives late Sunday into Monday and likely persists 
into mid week as upper troughiness sets up across eastern Canada and 
the northeastern CONUS. Shortwaves rotating around the upper trough 
will bring periods of showers and thunderstorms. These sorts of 
features are typically difficult to nail down in timing and strength 
this far out, and indeed, model solutions differ quite a bit from 
Sunday night onward. Have stayed with the NBM/WPC forecast for now 
given the uncertainty, which results in chances of 
showers/thunderstorms each day. Temperatures look to remain 
seasonable through the period.

KEY MESSAGE 2: High pressure will begin to drift east this weekend 
with more southerly to southwesterly flow, helping to advect warmer 
air into the region. Temperatures will warm into the low to mid 80s 
in general, with perhaps a few spot upper 80s in the Champlain 
Valley. Given an unfavorable moisture advection pattern, dewpoints 
should remain on the drier side for the weekend, remaining in the 
50s. Warm air advection will keep overnight temperatures on the mild 
side in the low to mid 60s. Subsidence under the high should keep 
any precipitation at bay through most of the weekend with mainly 
clear skies. Our next system is expected to swing through the area 
late Sunday into early next week. A cyclonic gyre over northern 
Quebec will bring troughiness to the East Coast, with a few 
shortwaves riding along the southern periphery of the gyre through 
the Great Lakes into our region. There is still quite the spread in 
the timing and phasing of any of these shortwaves, but trends have 
denoted best chances for precipitation to be Monday and Tuesday 
afternoons, which is in line with the current WPC forecast. As these 
waves pass through they will take with them small chunks of heat and 
moisture, gradually knocking our temperatures and humidity levels 
down into mid next week.

&&

.AVIATION /00Z THURSDAY THROUGH MONDAY/...
Through 00Z Friday...VFR conditions will prevail at all our TAF
sites this evening with breezy west to northwest winds
decreasing after sunset. A broken line of showers will move thru
the Champlain Valley in the next 1 to 2 hours before weakening
over northern/central VT by 02z. No flight restrictions are
expected with these showers. Given recent rainfall, thinking a
few hours of IFR/LIFR conditions are possible at SLK between
07-11z, so have tempo this into their taf for the potential of
bouncing IFR conditions. Probability is of IFR or lower 
conditions is 30 to 40%, given clouds/wind fields. Otherwise,
winds become 4 to 8 knots from the northwest tonight and
increase a little with VFR conditions prevailing on Thursday.

Outlook...

Thursday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Thursday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Friday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Friday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Saturday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Saturday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Sunday: VFR. Slight chance SHRA, Slight chance TSRA.

&&

.BTV WATCHES/WARNINGS/ADVISORIES...
VT...None.
NY...None.

&&

$$

WHAT HAS CHANGED...NWS BTV
DISCUSSION...NWS BTV
AVIATION...Taber
